data.title: Cathay General Bancorp Adopts New Share Repurchase Program >CATY
Bad Bunny was a hit for Calvin Klein - but not enough to shield profit outlook from tariffs
data.description:
By Bill Peters

PVH Corp., the parent of Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger, cuts its 2025 profit outlook, citing tariffs

Shares of PVH Corp. fell after hours on Wednesday after the Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger parent cut its full-year profit outlook, citing the impact of tariffs on shipments heading into the U.S.

Chief Executive Stefan Larsson, in a statement, said PVH (PVH) was "navigating an increasingly uncertain consumer and macroeconomic backdrop," adding "we're not yet fully able to offset that impact."

The forecast came as the company's first-quarter results topped Wall Street's expectations. Executives highlighted the viral ad campaign for a new underwear line earlier this year that featured rap megastar Bad Bunny, saying it was one of the company's "most impactful product launches in years."

PVH said it now expects adjusted earnings per share of $10.75 to $11 for its full year, down from an earlier outlook for $12.40 to $12.75. That outlook reflects "an estimated net negative impact related to the tariffs currently in place for goods coming into the U.S."

The company held to its revenue outlook over that time, which called for sales to land anywhere within a range of flat to a slight increase.

Shares were down 6.7% after hours on Wednesday.

"There is significant uncertainty with respect to global trade policies and the related impact on the broader macroeconomic environment, and, as such, the company's 2025 outlook could be subject to significant material change," PVH said.

Most clothing that people buy in the U.S. is brought in from elsewhere. PVH, in its most recent annual report, said that "Most of our products are imported into the countries where they are sold." Larsson said PVH would focus on its better-performing products and get bolder with its marketing - moves that could help in the second half of the year.

The company has tried to take steps to save money, including consolidating warehouses and its IT infrastructure. Along with staying competitive in Europe - which UBS analysts recently said was the company's most important market - PVH has tried to grow in North America and in Asia.

UBS analysts, in a note last week, said they were upbeat on PVH's longer-term goals. But they believed investors were pessimistic on the company overall.

Their analysis of quarterly results found that sales trends in Europe "softened sequentially, with only 7 out of 13 PVH's peers recently reporting positive sales growth in the region."

"Calvin Klein and Tommy Hilfiger are two of the world's strongest apparel brands," they said. "Both brands have high awareness, do a good job meeting customers' needs, and are on generally upward trajectories in our view."

They added that their own data "shows these brands could do a better job of engendering customer loyalty, but overall are well liked on both an absolute and a relative basis."

Over the past 12 months, PVH's stock has fallen 34.7%, as of the close of trade on Wednesday.

-Bill Peters

This content was created by MarketWatch, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. MarketWatch is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Trump plans to discontinue TSA's 'Quiet Skies' program, CBS News reports
data.description:
U.S. President Donald Trump plans to end the Transportation Security Administration's "Quiet Skies" surveillance program, a CBS News reporter said on Wednesday on X, citing unidentified sources.

The closure of the program could be announced as early as Thursday as aides have discussed how to shutter it without impacting security, CBS reported, citing unidentified U.S. officials.

The TSA declined to comment. The White House and Department of Homeland Security did not immediately respond to requests for comment.

Some Republican lawmakers had questioned the program earlier this year when U.S. Director of National Intelligence Tulsi Gabbard was placed on its watch list.

On Tuesday, CBS reported that Senator Jeanne Shaheen's husband was placed on the list in 2023 and was removed after she spoke with the TSA's then-director, CBS said.

According to CBS, the program employs analysts and undercover air marshals to identify and monitor travelers in airports and during flights who could pose a security risk.

CBS said that it was unclear if program staffers would be transferred elsewhere within the administration or if the air marshals' work would continue.

data.title: Fonterra's New Season Milk Price Forecast Reflects 'Considerable' Uncertainty
data.description:
Fonterra Co-operative Group's new season milk price forecast reflects "considerable" uncertainty amid the current geopolitical climate, BNZ economists said in a May 30 report.

The dairy group declared an opening forecast farmgate milk price of NZ$8 to NZ$11 per kilogram of milk solids (kgMS) for the 2025 to 2026 season, supported by stable near-term market demand.

BNZ expects NZ$9.50/kgMS for 2025 to 2026, assuming Global Dairy Trade (GDT) prices ease.

If prices remain stable, milk payouts could stay above NZ$10, making GDT results and the NZ dollar crucial indicators to monitor for the upcoming season, BNZ added.

data.title: Global Forex and Fixed Income Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering FX and Fixed Income. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2024 ET - Asian currencies consolidate against the dollar in the early session. However, Fed rate-cut prospects spurred by the disappointing U.S. economic data released overnight may support. Activity among U.S. services firms sank unexpectedly in May, according to the Institute for Supply Management's PMI. ADP National Employment Report showed only 37,000 jobs were created in May, the slowest pace of private-sector hiring in more than two years. U.S. money market pricing for a September Fed rate cut is now 97% priced, NAB's Head of FX Research Ray Attrill says, citing the weak data. USD/JPY is steady at 142.77; AUD/USD is little changed at 0.6494. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

2015 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as concerns about the strength of the U.S. economy grow following weak services activity and hiring data overnight. Carmakers are leading declines. Subaru Corp. is down 2.7% and Nissan Motor is 1.7% lower. USD/JPY is at 142.72, down sharply from 143.96 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching any developments related to U.S. tariffs and the war in Ukraine. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 0.4% at 37610.82. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

2008 ET - JGBs rise in the morning Tokyo session, tracking overnight price gains in U.S. Treasurys. JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. For today, market participants are likely to closely monitor the finance ministry's auction of about 800 billion yen in 30-year JGBs. This auction is expected to be digested smoothly, Citi Research's Tomohisa Fujiki says in a research report. It appears that pension funds, which have been holding back on purchases, are returning to the market, the rates strategist says. However, "investors may still need to keep an eye on the performance of the secondary market," Fujiki adds. The JGB 5-year yield is down 1.5 bps at 1.025%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

1945 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as concerns about the strength of U.S. economy grow following weak services-activity and hiring data overnight. Nikkei futures are down 0.7% at 37535 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 142.62, down from 143.96 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and the war in Ukraine. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.8% to 37747.45 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1543 ET - A string of disappointing data, coupled with a sobering Beige Book, drive Treasury yields to their lowest settle in nearly a month. ADP's jobs report was well below consensus, raising the prospect of a downside surprise on May payrolls due Friday. The report also prompted President Trump to again call for a Fed rate cut, but markets keep pricing a hold for now. May services PMI unexpectedly indicated contraction, for the first time since June 2024. The Fed report indicated the economy slowed substantially in May. Weekly jobless claims tomorrow are forecast to decrease a little. The 10-year sheds 0.095 percentage point to 4.364% and the two-year falls 0.080 p.p. to 3.877%. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1519 ET - Fixed income investment strategies favor liquid assets and investment-grade bonds, Nuveen's Tony Rodriguez says. Uncertainty has diminished a little since its peak two months ago around Liberation Day, but remains a hurdle for portfolio managers. Rodriguez forecasts a modest 1% GDP growth for the U.S. this year. That still makes him "pretty comfortable" with corporate and consumer credit fundamentals, but makes the economy more vulnerable to geopolitical shocks. He is venturing below investment grade, but avoiding the lowest quality credit. He likes leveraged loans and CLOs, for example. He also likes securities deemed less exposed to tariffs risk, such as municipal bonds and the financial sector's debt. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1452 ET - A decline in Treasury yields is likely the result of investors rejiggering bets on U.S. labor strength, Nuveen's Tony Rodriguez says. The ADP report printed 37,000, versus WSJ consensus of 110,000. The sharp yield decline that followed could reflect a realignment of expectations for Friday's payrolls, which are forecast to slow to 125,000 from 177,000. Investors now have reasons to believe that "the risks to the consensus…are more either balanced or to the downside," he said. Services PMI was also lower than forecast and just below 50, indicating contraction in the sector. Weak data challenge thoughts that 2Q activity could offset the 1Q contraction. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1341 ET - Efforts by Bank of Canada Governor Tiff Macklem to suggest the Canadian economy is not fundamentally weak "is foolhardy at best," says David Rosenberg, economist and head of Rosenberg Research. The BOC left its main interest rate unchanged, but Rosenberg says the excess slack accumulating in the labor market was reason enough to cut by a quarter point. The main argument from Macklem et al is that the economy has not weakened as much as feared so far, Rosenberg says. "The less the bank does now, the more it will have to do later," he tells clients. For now, Rosenberg says the BOC is in a waiting game in terms of addressing the tradeoffs on inflation from President Trump's trade policy. (Paul.Vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1248 ET - Borrowing in dollars to invest in some emerging-market currencies looks more appealing now that the U.S. economy is unlikely to grow fast and the greenback is weakening, TS Lombard's Daniel Von Ahlen writes. The carry trade is further supported by "the idea of a 'not too hot, not too cold' environment for risk appetite," he says. He favors the dollar as funding currency, but also likes the Chinese yuan and the Swiss franc, both low interest-rate currencies. Among the attractive target currencies, Von Ahlen lists the Brazilian real, the Mexican peso, the Colombian peso and the South African rand. "Economic surprises are currently strongest in EM," he says. (paulo.trevisani@wsj.com; @ptrevisani)

1232 ET - Bank of Canada Gov. Tiff Macklem says the economy is exhibiting "some resilience to tariffs," and the likelihood of a worst-case scenario stemming from U.S. trade policy has diminished. BOC kept its main rate unchanged at 2.75%, noting firmer-than-expected inflation and an economy that has softened but has not sharply weakened. Macklem says household consumption did slow in 1Q but continues to grow even though gauges of consumer confidence have dropped steeply. Macklem adds that business investment was "a bit stronger" than anticipated in 1Q. The stronger-than-expected economic performance, coupled with accelerating core inflation, provides BOC to hold off on further rate cuts despite distress in the labor market. (paul.vieira@wsj.com; @paulvieira)

1148 ET - The path for the Bank of Canada policy rate remains lower but it is uncertain where it will end up, Canadian Chamber of Commerce's Anderew DiCapua says. And that, the economist adds, adds more heat to their next decision in July. "It appears that uncertainty hung over the governing council." DiCapua says that with a more optimistic view of growth in the first quarter, the bank's council clearly were uneasy about where prices are headed. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

1109 ET - Oxford Economics' Michael Davenport reckons the Bank of Canada is in a bind as it weighs the downside risks to growth from the trade war with the U.S. against upside risks to inflation. Risks to inflation are coming through most notably from higher prices from tariffs, higher costs from supply chain strains, and the potential for rising inflation expectations. The economist expect the central bank and will continue to hold rates steady in July. He doesn't rule out a couple more quarter percentage point cuts this year. Davenport doesn't expect the policy rate will fall below 2.25% unless central bankers are convinced that inflation is under control and the economy needs more stimulus. (robb.stewart@wsj.com; @RobbMStewart)

data.title: Dongfeng Motor: Normal Production, Operational Activities of Company Won't Be Affected
NZ Commerce Commission Proposes to Reduce Supplier Charges by Supermarkets
data.description:
New Zealand's Commerce Commission proposed changes to limit the range of charges that major supermarkets, including Woolworths Group's New Zealand stores, can impose on their suppliers, according to a Thursday statement by the regulator.

The proposal is part of a draft review of the Grocery Supply Code, aimed at addressing the power imbalance between dominant supermarket chains and small suppliers.

The regulator said suppliers are often forced to absorb costs such as shelf-stocking and in-store promotions due to fear of losing access to supermarket shelves.

The commission also raised concerns about promotional payments in the wholesale market, which it says give major retailers a pricing edge that competitors cannot match.

The watchdog also recommends that major supermarkets expand their wholesale ranges and pass on promotional funding to wholesale customers. It also urges suppliers to reduce reliance on promotional deals or share them more broadly across retailers.

It is encouraging voluntary reforms but signals regulatory action if no meaningful progress is made within a year.

Submissions on the draft code are open, with a final report due by the end of September.

"There's always room for improvement, and we're talking to our suppliers all the time so we can keep doing better," Woolworths New Zealand's Interim Managing Director Pieter said in a Thursday statement by the retailer commenting on the regulatory proposal.

data.title: Trump Pressures Fed Powell to Cut Rates After Weak Jobs Report
data.description:
President Donald Trump on Wednesday lashed out at Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, demanding rate cuts just after ADP reported the weakest private sector job growth since March 2023.

On Truth Social, Trump wrote, “Europe has lowered nine times!”.

Their recent White House meeting reportedly turned tense.

According to press secretary Karoline Leavitt, Trump told Powell he was “making a mistake by not lowering interest rates,” arguing it puts the U.S. at a disadvantage to countries like China.

The Fed stated Powell emphasized that policy should follow economic data, not politics.

Since returning to office, Trump has repeatedly attacked Powell, calling him a “major loser” and nicknaming him “Too Late” over rate decisions.

Though Trump hinted at firing Powell before his term ends in 2026, he said in April he had “no intention” of doing so—yet continues to air frustrations publicly.

data.title: KALA BIO Executives Sell Shares
data.description:
Reporter NameRelationshipTypeAmountSEC Filing
Iwicki Mark TDirectorSell$40,638Form 4
Brazzell Romulus KOfficerSell$13,789Form 4
Bazemore ToddOfficerSell$12,678Form 4
Reumuth MaryCHIEF FINANCIAL OFFICERSell$10,659Form 4

Several executives at KALA BIO have recently sold shares of the company's common stock, according to SEC Form 4 filings.

Mark T Iwicki, a Director at KALA BIO, sold 10,866 shares at a weighted average price of $3.74 per share, totaling $40,638. Following this transaction, Iwicki directly owns 269,210 shares, including 154,042 unvested RSUs.

Romulus K Brazzell, Head of Research and Development and Chief Medical Officer, sold 3,687 shares at the same price, totaling $13,789. Post-sale, Brazzell directly owns 87,949 shares, with 52,402 being unvested RSUs. This sale was executed under a 10b5-1 trading plan for tax obligations related to RSU vesting.

Todd Bazemore, serving as Interim CEO, President, and COO, sold 3,390 shares at a weighted average price of $3.74, totaling $12,678. Bazemore now directly owns 87,757 shares and indirectly owns 1 share through his son. This transaction was also part of a pre-arranged 10b5-1 trading plan for tax obligations from vested RSUs.

Mary Reumuth, the CFO, sold 2,850 shares at the same price, totaling $10,659. Following her transaction, Reumuth directly owns 65,731 shares, including 40,953 unvested restricted stock units.

data.title: Japan’s Nominal Wage Growth Misses Forecasts in April
data.description:
Nominal wages in Japan rose 2.3% year-on-year in April 2025, matching the pace seen in March but falling short of market expectations of a 2.6% increase.

Meanwhile, real wages—adjusted for inflation and a key indicator of consumer purchasing power—declined by 1.8%, marking the fourth consecutive month of contraction.

The weak real wage data highlights the ongoing challenge posed by persistent inflation, which continues to outpace the pay increases granted by employers.

This is despite major Japanese firms committing to wage hikes of over 5% during the annual spring labor negotiations in March.

The subdued wage growth adds to concerns over Japan’s economic outlook, especially amid rising global uncertainty fueled by recent US tariffs.

It also complicates the Bank of Japan’s efforts to normalize monetary policy, as stagnant real incomes may weigh on consumer spending and hinder broader economic recovery.

data.title: BarkBox Apologizes for Leaked Message Suspending Pride Marketing — WSJ
data.description:
By Patrick Coffee

BarkBox's chief executive apologized after a leaked internal Slack message revealed that the subscription service that provides dog products for pet owners would pause advertising its Pride merchandise in deference to the political environment.

"Right now," the Slack message read in part, "pushing this promo risks unintentionally sending the message that 'we're not for you' to a large portion of our audience."

"I do not agree with the content of the message," founder and CEO Matt Meeker wrote Wednesday on BarkBox's social-media accounts. "It wasn't good, it doesn't reflect our values, and I'm deeply sorry that it happened."

Many advertisers are rethinking their Pride month marketing as President Trump cracks down on diversity policies and transgender rights, conservative activists encourage boycotts of offending companies and activist investors try to convince shareholders that LGBTQ marketing is partisan and divisive.

They also remember the damaging boycotts two years ago over Pride merchandise at retailer Target and beer brand Bud Light's collaboration with a transgender influencer. Target more recently has faced a boycott by shoppers upset over its retreat from DEI.

Some longtime supporters of New York City's Pride March, including Mastercard, Citi, PepsiCo and Nissan, this year chose not to renew their corporate sponsorships.

Last month, Jenna Ellis, a former attorney for President Trump, posted a screenshot on X opting out of both of her monthly BarkBox delivery boxes for the month of June, writing, "I do not want Pride boxes as it goes against my religious beliefs."

BarkBox doesn't include its Pride products as part of its regular monthly subscription packages, but it has sold them as add-ons for the past four years alongside other themed collections such as "4th of July" and "Backyard BBQ." Posts poking fun at the products went viral on social media several weeks ago, with one TikTok user reading BarkBox descriptions of toys including "Slay the Dragon Queen," a stuffed dragon with rainbow wings, and "Clamantha," a plush clam who's dating a toy tennis ball named Pearl.

The internal Slack message announcing a pause in Pride marketing, a screenshot of which made its way onto social media this week, said the current climate makes "this promotion feel more like a political statement than a universally joyful moment for all dog people."

If BarkBox wouldn't be comfortable promoting "a MAGA-themed product," the message argued, "it's worth asking whether this is the right moment to run this particular campaign."

Meeker spoke up after the Slack screenshot led to confusion regarding the company's position on Pride marketing, according to a BarkBox spokesman. The CEO's post doesn't address the subject directly, noting only that the Pride collection remains for sale on BarkBox's home page.

The company will donate 100% of the revenue the products generate to a charity supporting the LGBTQ community, up from a partial donation in earlier years, Meeker added. The charity is Kaleidoscope Youth Center, an Ohio nonprofit supporting LGBTQ youth and young adults, the spokesman said.

The spokesman declined to comment on whether BarkBox would actively market the Pride collection or not.

The event illustrates how easily people can use an intensely polarized climate to target brands online by politicizing anything, even pet supplies, according to Ari Lightman, a professor of digital media and marketing at Carnegie Mellon University.

"I call it the outrage culture: 'I am outraged by this, this has hurt my moral sensibilities, and I want to let others know. In doing so, I am going to harm the company,' " said Lightman. "And they can do it."

Ellis on Wednesday responded to BarkBox's apology by posting the Slack screenshot on X and writing that she was canceling her subscription because Meeker was "weakly apologizing instead of listening to his leadership AND his customers."

"He should have learned from the mistakes of Bud Light and Target," she wrote.

Write to Patrick Coffee at patrick.coffee@wsj.com

data.title: Trump Loves Europe's Rate Cuts. Why the Fed Should Take Cover. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Brian Swint

President Donald Trump reignited his campaign to get Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell to lower interest rates, holding up the European Central Bank as an example of how it should be done.

Well, the ECB is widely expected to lower rates again Thursday for an eighth consecutive meeting, bringing the key rate down by two percentage points since this time last year. The Fed, by contrast, delivered one point of cuts over the course of three meetings starting in September and has been on pause since December.

Trump noted those divergent paths after economic data showed weak U.S. hiring in May — ADP's private payrolls report showed 37,000 jobs were added in May, down from a revised 60,000 in April.

"'Too Late' Powell must now LOWER THE RATE. He is unbelievable!!!" Trump posted on social media. He noted that Europe had already lowered rates more.

The Fed declined to comment when contacted by Barron's.

To be fair to Powell, the decisions for the ECB may have been more clear-cut, since the effects of macro uncertainty and the impact of Trump's tariffs are more likely to damp prices in Europe while weighing on economic growth. By contrast, the tariffs may well fan inflation in the U.S. by increasing the cost of doing business.

Inflation in the 20-nation euro area slowed to 1.9% in May, below the ECB's 2% target. The Fed's preferred gauge of inflation has been declining but it's still well above its goal — core PCE came in at 2.5% in April, according to data released last week.

"Weak inflation, the hit to euro area growth from U.S. tariffs on the European Union, and the risk of disinflationary spillovers in Europe from U.S. tariffs on China will see the ECB continue to cut rates," said Nomura economist Andrzej Szczepaniak. He sees the main ECB rate falling to 1.5% from the current 2.25% by September.

ECB President Christine Lagarde will hold a press conference in Frankfurt to explain the decision. The big question is how much further policy makers will be able to lower borrowing costs--some have argued that rates are low enough to not be restrictive to the economy and have opposed dropping them much further.

Markets are pricing in a near certainty that the ECB will bring its key rate to 2% on Thursday, according to LSEG data. The next quarter-point cut from the Fed — bringing the key U.S. rate to 4%-4.25% — isn't expected until September, the CME FedWatch tool shows.

In any case, if the Fed does lower interest rates later this year, it will be playing catchup with the ECB.

Write to Brian Swint at brian.swint@barrons.com

This content was created by Barron's, which is operated by Dow Jones & Co. Barron's is published independently from Dow Jones Newswires and The Wall Street Journal.

data.title: Global Equities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Equities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

2015 ET - Japanese stocks are lower as concerns about the strength of the U.S. economy grow following weak services activity and hiring data overnight. Carmakers are leading declines. Subaru Corp. is down 2.7% and Nissan Motor is 1.7% lower. USD/JPY is at 142.72, down sharply from 143.96 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are closely watching any developments related to U.S. tariffs and the war in Ukraine. The Nikkei Stock Average is down 0.4% at 37610.82. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com; @kosakunarioka)

1945 ET - Japanese stocks may fall as concerns about the strength of U.S. economy grow following weak services-activity and hiring data overnight. Nikkei futures are down 0.7% at 37535 on the SGX. USD/JPY is at 142.62, down from 143.96 as of Wednesday's Tokyo stock market close. Investors are focusing on any developments related to U.S. tariffs and the war in Ukraine. The Nikkei Stock Average rose 0.8% to 37747.45 on Wednesday. (kosaku.narioka@wsj.com)

1943 ET - Elders's purchase of Delta Agribusiness for a A$475 million enterprise value is ultimately likely to happen, says Jefferies. That is despite the Australian Competition & Consumer Commission raising initial concerns about the deal. Analyst John Campbell thinks Elders will need to sell some assets to secure ACCC approval. Jefferies also thinks allowing Elders to get bigger through M&A could benefit Australian farmers. It would help Elders to compete more effectively with Nutrien, which has significant global fertilizer manufacturing capabilities that give it a competitive advantage, Jefferies says. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1938 ET - The takeover tussle for PointsBet just got more interesting. MIXI Australia has raised its offer for PointsBet by 13% to A$1.20/share. Also, MIXI could make an off-market bid if it is unsuccessful with its Scheme Implementation Deed. MIXI's offer price now matches the all-cash bid by betr Entertainment. "The ball now appears to be in betr's court though the ability to increase its bid seems limited given funding constraints or limitations," Bell Potter analyst Chris Savage says. Betr owns 19.9% of PointsBet, which means MIXI is unlikely to succeed with the Scheme. This explains why MIXI is considering proceeding with an off-market offer if the Scheme is not approved, Bell Potter says. "Hence this may still have a way to play out," it adds. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1926 ET [Dow Jones]--Car dealer Autosports's new debt facility and improving earnings momentum help cement Wilsons's bullish view of its stock. Autosports yesterday announced a new A$350 million syndicated debt facility with four existing financiers. Analyst James Ferrier says it gives Autosports more firepower for M&A when car manufacturers are rethinking their dealership arrangements. "Looking ahead, falling interest rates and the potential unwind of the luxury car tax present demand tailwinds," Wilsons says. It expects gross margins to improve across many car brands that Autosports sells. (david.winning@wsj.com)

1921 ET - Military shipbuilder Austal's share price has rallied more than 90% since the start of this year. That prompts Bell Potter to downgrade the stock to "hold," from "buy." "We remain positive on the long-term outlook for Austal, considering the macro tailwinds and attractive growth profile," analyst Daniel Laing says. "However, we are conscious of its current valuation." Bell Potter raises its price target by 26%, to A$5.60/share. But that remains below Austal's closing price of A$5.94 on Wednesday. Key risks to Bell Potter's downgrade include more passive funds flows from Austal's potential inclusion in the ASX 200 index. Bell Potter also highlights the potential for material contract awards linked to the Strategic Shipbuilding Agreement with the Australian government. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1843 ET - It's only a matter of time before Greatland Gold acquires Antipa Minerals, reckons Euroz Hartleys. That's because Antipa's Minyari Gold project is located just 22 miles from Greatland Gold's Telfer processing plant in Australia. Analyst Michael Scantlebury notes Greatland's updated outlook for Telfer, which lays out how it expects to bridge the gap between now and when production starts from the Havieron deposit in FY 2028. Euroz Hartleys believes there would be ample processing capacity for Antipa's annual output of 3 million tons of ore from FY 2028. It also thinks "Antipa's Minyari Gold project ore tons are higher margin and lower risk" than what Greatland currently envisages. Euroz Hartleys lifts its price target on Antipa by 23% to A$0.98/share. Antipa ended Wednesday at A$0.66. (david.winning@wsj.com; @dwinningWSJ)

1833 ET [Dow Jones]--Microsoft's leadership shakeup demonstrates its confidence that agentic artificial intelligence is making software-as-a-service obsolete, an analyst says. Microsoft put its Office products under the purview of LinkedIn's CEO, which Moor Insights analyst Patrick Moorhead says will consolidate its office applications with its AI agent business. That will help Microsoft build AI more efficiently, as it has said it wants to shift away from SaaS, Moor says. Microsoft will also look more appealing to customers transitioning to AI, he says. "If a tech company wants to replace SaaS, the better-suited company is likely the one with both SaaS apps and agents." (katherine.hamilton@wsj.com)

1829 ET - Australia's S&P/ASX 200 looks set for an uncertain start to Thursday's session after U.S. indices finished mixed. ASX futures are down by less than 0.1% ahead of the open, suggesting that the benchmark index may struggle to make the 0.2% gain it needs to close at a record. The ASX 200 has rebounded more than 15% from its tariff-driven April dip and is coming off a 0.9% rise. In the U.S., bonds rallied and stocks were mixed as weak economic data boosted confidence of Fed rate cuts over coming months. The DJIA slipped 0.2%, the S&P 500 added less than 0.1%, and the Nasdaq Composite rose 0.3%. (stuart.condie@wsj.com)

1634 ET - Starbucks's newly appointed chief stores officer is already getting a promotion, with Mike Grams named as the coffee giant's COO. Grams, who previously worked for 30 years at Taco Bell, joined Starbucks in February as CEO Brian Niccol, also a Taco Bell alum, assembled his new leadership team. Starbucks last had a COO in 2022. Niccol is working to turn around the chain's sales, and Grams will have an expanded role in overseeing global store operations, development and supply chain. (heather.haddon@wsj.com ; @heatherhaddon)

1605 ET - U.S. stocks end narrowly mixed as ISM services activity unexpectedly contracted in May, while ADP data showed just 37,000 jobs were created, the slowest pace of private-sector hiring in more than two years. This prompts President Trump to repeat his call for interest rate cuts, while he also says his Chinese counterpart, Xi Jinping, was tough to make a trade deal with. Communications services is the best performing sector, led by gains in Meta Platforms, while energy falls the most as oil prices decline. DJIA falls 91 points, or 0.2%, to 42427, the S&P 500 is little moved at 5970, while the Nasdaq climbs 0.3% to 19460. (patrick.sullivan@wsj.com)

1410 ET - TD Cowen says Uranium Energy Corp. continues to ramp-up its Christensen Ranch mine in Wyoming with new production areas coming online "that we expect will mark the transition to producer status in FQ4/25." Analyst Craig Hutchison says in a report that this coincides with the renewed focus on the US domestic nuclear supply chain including homegrown production. Hutchison adds that Uranium Energy is one of the "very few" pure-play uranium companies with near-term production in a politically stable jurisdiction with potential for additional US government support. (adriano.marchese@wsj.com)

data.title: Dongfeng Motor: Controlling Shareholder Says It Isn't Involved in Any Restructuring of Business for the Moment
Coronado Global Resources: in Confidential Talks With Queensland Electricity Provider Stanwell
Oil Falls on Report That Saudi Arabia Favors More Production Increases — Market Talk
data.description: Oil edges lower in the early Asian session. There's a report "indicating that Saudi Arabia is exerting considerable pressure for a more aggressive increase in oil supply by OPEC+," XS.com's Antonio Di Giacomo says. The "kingdom is actively seeking to regain market share, a goal that is driving its desire to accelerate production increases in the coming months," says the financial markets analyst for LATAM. The country's strategy focuses on getting OPEC+ to boost production by at least 411,000 barrels per day in August and September, the analyst adds. Front-month WTI crude oil futures are down 0.3% at $62.65/bbl; front-month Brent crude oil futures are 0.1% lower at $64.79/bbl. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: JGBs Rise, Tracking Gains in U.S. Treasurys — Market Talk
data.description: JGBs rise in the morning Tokyo session, tracking overnight price gains in U.S. Treasurys. JGBs and Treasurys tend to move in tandem. For today, market participants are likely to closely monitor the finance ministry's auction of about 800 billion yen in 30-year JGBs. This auction is expected to be digested smoothly, Citi Research's Tomohisa Fujiki says in a research report. It appears that pension funds, which have been holding back on purchases, are returning to the market, the rates strategist says. However, "investors may still need to keep an eye on the performance of the secondary market," Fujiki adds. The JGB 5-year yield is down 1.5 bps at 1.025%. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)
